title: Kinematics of uniform circular motion
summary: This lesson introduces the fundamental kinematic concepts of uniform circular motion. It covers angular displacement, angular velocity, and the crucial concept of centripetal acceleration, which arises even when an object's speed is constant. Essential formulae linking linear and angular quantities are derived and applied.
learningObjectives: Define and use angular displacement (θ) in radians and angular velocity (ω).
Recall and use the relationship between linear speed and angular velocity, v = rω.
Understand that an object in uniform circular motion is accelerating, and derive and use the formulae for centripetal acceleration, a = v²/r and a = rω².

simpleExplanation.title: The Merry-Go-Round's Secret
simpleExplanation.summary: An object moving in a circle at a steady speed is constantly changing its direction. This change in direction means its velocity is changing, which requires an acceleration directed towards the centre of the circle.
simpleExplanation.analogy: Imagine you're driving a car around a roundabout at a constant 20 mph. Even though your speedometer doesn't change, you have to keep the steering wheel turned. This constant turning action is like a continuous 'nudge' towards the centre of the roundabout, changing your car's direction. This 'nudge' is caused by a force, which means there must be an acceleration.
steps: Define angular velocity, ω, as the rate of change of angle (in radians) per second. It's how fast something is spinning.
Recognise that while speed is constant, the velocity vector is always changing direction, pointing tangentially to the circle.
This change in velocity vector means there is an acceleration. This acceleration is always directed towards the centre of the circle.
Use the formulae a = v²/r or a = rω² to calculate the magnitude of this centripetal acceleration in exam problems.

flashcards.back: The angle subtended at the centre of a circle by an arc that is equal in length to the radius of the circle. 2π radians = 360°.
The angle in radians through which a point or line has been rotated about a specified axis. It is a vector quantity.
The rate of change of angular displacement with time. ω = Δθ / Δt. Units: rad s⁻¹.

flashcards.front: What is the formula for angular velocity in terms of period, T?
flashcards.back: ω = 2π / T. This is because for one full revolution, the angle is 2π radians and the time taken is the period T.

flashcards.front: What is the formula for angular velocity in terms of frequency, f?
flashcards.back: ω = 2πf. This follows from ω = 2π / T and the fact that frequency f = 1/T.

flashcards.front: How are linear speed (v) and angular velocity (ω) related?
flashcards.back: v = rω, where r is the radius of the circular path. The linear speed is the tangential speed of the object.
The acceleration of an object moving in uniform circular motion. It is always directed towards the centre of the circle and is responsible for changing the direction of the velocity.

flashcards.front: What are the two main formulae for centripetal acceleration (a)?
flashcards.back: a = v²/r and a = rω². Use the one that is most convenient for the data given in the problem.

flashcards.front: An object moves in a circle at a constant speed. Is its acceleration zero?
flashcards.back: No. Its speed is constant, but its velocity is not, because the direction is continuously changing. This change in velocity means there is an acceleration (centripetal acceleration).

flashcards.front: What is the angle between the velocity vector and the acceleration vector for an object in uniform circular motion?
flashcards.back: 90 degrees (or π/2 radians). The velocity is tangential to the circle, and the acceleration is radial (points to the centre).

flashcards.front: How do you convert from revolutions per minute (rpm) to rad s⁻¹?
flashcards.back: Multiply by 2π (to convert revolutions to radians) and divide by 60 (to convert minutes to seconds). So, 1 rpm = (2π/60) rad s⁻¹.

faq.q: If the speed is constant, how can there be acceleration? I thought acceleration was a change in speed.
faq.a: Acceleration is defined as the rate of change of *velocity*. Velocity is a vector, so it has both magnitude (speed) and direction. In uniform circular motion, the magnitude (speed) is constant, but the direction is always changing. Any change in velocity, including a change in direction only, constitutes an acceleration.

faq.q: What's the real difference between angular velocity (ω) and linear velocity (v)?
faq.a: Imagine a spinning vinyl record. Every point on the record completes a circle in the same amount of time, so every point has the same angular velocity (ω). However, a point on the outer edge has to travel a much larger circumference than a point near the centre. To cover this larger distance in the same time, the outer point must have a greater linear velocity (v). So, ω is about the rate of rotation, while v is the actual speed of a point along its path.

faq.q: Is centripetal force a new type of force like gravity or friction?
faq.a: No, and this is a crucial point. Centripetal force is not a fundamental force of nature. It is the *resultant force* that is *required* to cause circular motion. The name 'centripetal force' is just a label we give to the net force pointing towards the centre. This force could be provided by gravity (for a planet), tension (for a ball on a string), friction (for a car on a roundabout), or the magnetic force (for a charged particle in a B-field).

sections.content: From the orbit of a planet around its star to a car navigating a roundabout, or an electron in a magnetic field, circular motion is a fundamental concept in physics. In this lesson, we will develop the language and equations needed to describe objects moving in a circle at a constant speed, a situation known as uniform circular motion. You will discover the surprising fact that 'constant speed' does not mean 'zero acceleration'.
1. Measuring Angles in Physics: The Radian

While degrees are common in daily life, physicists and mathematicians prefer to use radians for measuring angles. A radian is defined based on the geometry of a circle itself, which simplifies many physics equations. One radian (1 rad) is the angle subtended at the centre of a circle by an arc with a length equal to the circle's radius. Since the circumference of a circle is $2\pi r$, a full circle of 360° corresponds to $2\pi$ radians.

items: 1 radian is the angle where arc length $s$ equals radius $r$.
The formula linking arc length, radius, and angle is $s = r\theta$, where $\theta$ must be in radians.

sections.content: Just as linear displacement describes a change in position along a straight line, angular displacement, $\theta$, describes the angle an object has rotated through. The rate at which this angle changes is called the angular velocity, $\omega$ (omega). For uniform circular motion, the angular velocity is constant.

The unit for angular velocity is radians per second (rad s⁻¹). For one complete revolution, the angular displacement is $2\pi$ radians and the time taken is the period, $T$. This gives a very useful relationship: $\omega = 2\pi / T$. Since frequency, $f$, is $1/T$, we also have $\omega = 2\pi f$.

sections.question: A hard disk in a computer spins at 7200 revolutions per minute (rpm). Calculate its angular velocity in rad s⁻¹.
sections.solution:
1. **Convert revolutions to radians:** One revolution is $2\pi$ radians. So, 7200 revolutions is $7200 \times 2\pi$ radians.
2. **Convert minutes to seconds:** One minute is 60 seconds.
3. **Calculate angular velocity:**
   $\omega = \frac{\text{angle}}{\text{time}} = \frac{7200 \times 2\pi \text{ rad}}{60 \text{ s}}$
   $\omega = 120 \times 2\pi \text{ rad s}^{-1}$
   $\omega = 240\pi \text{ rad s}^{-1}$
   $\omega \approx 754 \text{ rad s}^{-1}$ (to 3 s.f.)

[1 mark for conversion, 1 mark for correct calculation and answer]

sections.content: This is the most important, and perhaps most counter-intuitive, concept. According to Newton's First Law, an object's velocity is constant unless a resultant force acts on it. Velocity is a vector, having both magnitude (speed) and direction. For an object in uniform circular motion, the speed is constant, but the direction of motion is continuously changing. A change in velocity is, by definition, acceleration. Because this acceleration is responsible for changing the direction of the velocity to keep it on a circular path, it must always be directed towards the centre of the circle. This is called centripetal acceleration.

By considering the change in the velocity vector over a small time interval, it can be shown that the magnitude of this centripetal acceleration, $a$, depends on the object's linear speed, $v$, and the radius of the circle, $r$. We can also express this in terms of angular velocity, $\omega$.

items: Centripetal acceleration is caused by a change in the direction of velocity, not its magnitude.
The acceleration vector always points towards the centre of the circular path.
The acceleration vector is always perpendicular to the instantaneous velocity vector (which is tangential to the path).

sections.content: Examiners love to test the vector nature of circular motion. A common question asks you to draw vectors for velocity and acceleration on a diagram of an object moving in a circle. Always draw the velocity vector as a tangent to the circle and the acceleration vector pointing from the object directly to the centre of the circle. They must be perpendicular.

sections.question:
The Moon orbits the Earth in a circle of approximate radius $3.84 \times 10^8$ m. The time taken for one orbit is 27.3 days. Calculate:
(a) the angular velocity of the Moon.
(b) the linear speed of the Moon.
(c) the centripetal acceleration of the Moon.
sections.solution:
(a) **Calculate angular velocity, ω:**
First, convert the period T into seconds:
$T = 27.3 \text{ days} \times 24 \text{ hours/day} \times 3600 \text{ s/hour} = 2.3587 \times 10^6$ s
Now use the formula $\omega = 2\pi / T$:
$\omega = \frac{2\pi}{2.3587 \times 10^6 \text{ s}} = 2.66 \times 10^{-6} \text{ rad s}^{-1}$

(b) **Calculate linear speed, v:**
Use the formula $v = r\omega$:
$v = (3.84 \times 10^8 \text{ m}) \times (2.66 \times 10^{-6} \text{ rad s}^{-1}) = 1021 \text{ m s}^{-1}$
$v \approx 1.02 \times 10^3 \text{ m s}^{-1}$ (to 3 s.f.)

(c) **Calculate centripetal acceleration, a:**
We can use either formula. Using $a = r\omega^2$ is often better as it uses the value of $\omega$ calculated in part (a) without relying on the calculated speed from (b).
$a = r\omega^2 = (3.84 \times 10^8 \text{ m}) \times (2.66 \times 10^{-6} \text{ rad s}^{-1})^2$
$a = 2.72 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m s}^{-2}$
Alternatively, using $a = v^2/r$:
$a = \frac{(1021 \text{ m s}^{-1})^2}{3.84 \times 10^8 \text{ m}} = 2.71 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m s}^{-2}$
(The small difference is due to rounding.) The direction of this acceleration is always towards the centre of the Earth.

sections.label: Practice Questions: Kinematics of Uniform Circular Motion
